narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your family's low income bracket, and we appreciate the courage it takes to get this far. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated your risk of failure at 42%, which is high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—and that stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward expecting a struggle on the bar exam. On a more positive note, your low family income actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success in a way that surprised the model compared to higher-income applicants who often perform similarly. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly below the average, also helped pull the odds toward passing, showing the model some resilience in your academic record. Even your Year 1 decile in the second bottom tenth, LSAT score of 29, and full-time student status all contributed positively, though less forcefully, placing you in line with many who still succeed. Overall, while most features leaned toward you passing, that one standout low Year 3 performance overshadowed them, making your profile riskier than the typical admitted student's stronger, more consistent rankings and higher test scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many students with similar starts have turned things around. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or tutoring, retake the LSAT to aim above the average 36.7, and highlight any bar prep or leadership experiences that demonstrate growth. Consider part-time programs or bridge courses if full-time feels overwhelming, and seek mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated similar paths. You're not defined by this prediction, and with strategic steps, you can rewrite your story for the next opportunity. Keep pushing forward—we believe in you.
